summary: Gruber argues that Adobe's claim of Flash being 'open' doesn't hold up under scrutiny. He establishes that openness exists on a continuum, positioning HTML5 as genuinely open because no single vendor controls its specification or implementation. He then dismantles Adobe's openness claim by noting that their published SWF spec is incomplete — non-Adobe implementations can't achieve feature parity due to DRM restrictions. His coup de grâce is a reductio ad absurdum: if publishing a file format spec makes Flash 'open,' then Microsoft Office is even more open, since OOXML was submitted to and accepted by ISO — something Adobe never did with SWF.
key_points: Openness is a continuum, not binary — the debate is about where thresholds lie on that spectrum
HTML5 qualifies as open because no single vendor controls its spec or implementations, and all five major browser makers agree on it
Adobe's published SWF spec is incomplete — open-source Flash implementations like Gnash can't achieve parity because DRM components are withheld
By Adobe's own standard of openness (publishing a file format spec), Microsoft Office qualifies as even more open since OOXML is an ISO standard
The distinction between non-Adobe implementations that 'don't' work versus ones that 'can't' work is critical to the openness argument
money_quotes: "Open" is one of those terms that means a lot of different things to different people.
Open-vs.-closed is a continuum — shades of gray, not just black and white.
There's not much you can get those five to agree upon.
It's not just that non-Adobe implementations don't work, but that they can't.
I often find it a useful technique to concede a point for the sake of argument and see where that leads.
Anyone who argues that Adobe Flash is open would agree that Microsoft Office is even more open.
key_insight: Adobe's claim that Flash is 'open' because they published a partial file format spec inadvertently concedes that Microsoft Office — with its ISO-standardized OOXML format — is even more open, exposing the hollowness of the argument.
